data.title: What does the DICK's/Foot Locker merger mean for Nike's European rivals?
data.description:
The $2.4B  buyout of Foot Locker (NYSE:FL) by DICK’s Sporting Goods (NYSE:DKS) has the potential to reshape the sporting goods retail space in the U.S. as DICK’s (NYSE:DKS) can now capture the below-average income consumer, broadening its customer base and making DICK’s (NYSE:DKS) the dominant player in the sportswear/sporting goods category.

This is good news for Nike (NYSE:NKE). As the top-selling brand at both Foot Locker (NYSE:FL) and DICK’s Sporting Goods (DKS), a merger can only accelerate growth for Nike (NYSE:NKE), most likely at the expense of its European competitors.

With 30-40% U.S. market share, Nike (NYSE:NKE) currently accounts for 25% shelf space at DICK’s (DKS) and makes up a little over half of Foot Locker’s (NYSE:FL) merchandise (although this was down from 63% in 2023).

This concentration may not be ideal for adidas (OTCQX:ADDYY) (OTCQX:ADDDF) which has been trying to expand its shelf space among U.S. retailers, taking advantage of Nike’s (NKE) strategic shift towards online sales. 

“In the near-term, the impact [of a DKS/FL merger on adidas] may be limited, as retailers are likely to prioritize brands showing strong momentum, but over the longer-term, this development could make it more difficult for adidas to win share in the U.S.,” says UBS retail analyst Robert Krankowski.

For Puma (OTCPK:PMMAF), the impact could be magnified by its smaller U.S. market share. Although Puma (OTCPK:PMMAF) has shown significant growth in the U.S., it still only holds a 4% market share versus 7% for adidas (ADDYY, ADDDF).

Another interested party in the DKS/FL merger is UK’s sportswear retailer JD Sports (OTCPK:JDSPY). Krankowski believes the 86.5% premium paid for Foot Locker (FL) could be viewed as a reference point for other potential mergers in the sporting goods retail space.

Other than that, “another major competitor [with a combined market share of 25% for DKS and FL] increasing its exposure to Nike products could pose further challenges for JD Sports in delivering sustainable outperformance in North America,” Krankowski says.

data.title: Senators are said to renew push to contest Big Tech’s hold on Pentagon deals
data.description:
A bipartisan duo in the Senate is reviving legislation aimed at loosening the stronghold that major tech firms -- such as Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) -- have on the Defense Department’s cloud and artificial intelligence deals.

The bill, known as the Protecting AI and Cloud Competition in Defense Act, will be reintroduced Thursday by Democratic Senator Elizabeth Warren of Massachusetts and Republican Senator Eric Schmitt of Missouri, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the effort. A companion version is also expected to be unveiled in the House, marking its debut in that chamber.

The proposed legislation seeks to open the playing field for contracts valued at $50 million or more by requiring competitive bidding processes and removing structural obstacles that prevent smaller tech companies and emerging vendors from participating. The ultimate aim is to diversify the Defense Department’s vendor base and foster innovation beyond the traditional Silicon Valley powerhouses.

Lawmakers are increasingly navigating the tension between advancing federal adoption of AI and avoiding further concentration of power among the biggest tech companies. Warren, who sits on the Senate Armed Services Committee along with Schmitt, underscored the national security stakes involved.

“It’s a mistake to let Silicon Valley monopolize our AI and cloud computing tools because it doesn’t just stifle innovation, it increases costs and threatens our national security,” Warren said in a statement.

Originally introduced in December during the Biden administration’s final weeks, the bill didn’t advance, but has gained renewed momentum in light of recent White House procurement guidelines aimed at fostering a more competitive AI ecosystem.

data.title: American Express credit card charge-offs drop in April, delinquencies stable
data.description:
American Express (NYSE:AXP) consumer credit card delinquencies held stable in April, while its net charge-offs improved and its lending activity increased, according to data the company released on Thursday.

Its U.S. consumer card delinquency rate stayed at 1.4%, the same level it has been at for at least three months. That's also unchanged from its April 2024 level, as well as April 2019, before the pandemic disrupted the economy.

Its net charge-off rate of 2.0% declined from 2.4% in March and 2.5% in April 2024. That also compared favorably with 2.4% in April 2019.

U.S. consumer card total loans outstanding were $90.7B, up 0.7% M/M and 9.8% Y/Y.

American Express's (NYSE:AXP) U.S. small business card delinquency rate of 1.6% was unchanged from March, and its net charge-off rate of 2.4% ticked down from 2.6% in the prior month.

U.S. small business card loans of $31.6B as of April 30, 2025, climbed from $31.2B at March 31 and from $30.2B at Feb. 28.

data.title: Alcoa cut at UBS as valuation not compelling with alumina 'down and out'
data.description:
Alcoa (NYSE:AA) -2.6% in Thursday's trading as UBS downgraded shares to Neutral from Buy, as the bank remains constructive on the medium-term outlook for aluminum prices but believes alumina likely will remain lower for longer.

UBS analysts led by Lachlan Shaw said they expect upside in London Metal Exchange aluminum will be capped by softening demand due to trade war uncertainty affecting demand and a reversal of pre-tariff buying.

Alcoa (NYSE:AA) is more exposed to aluminum than alumina and could benefit from a reduction in U.S. tariffs, but removal of Section 232 is not the base case, UBS said, and after a ~25% bounce from recent lows vs. more limited gains in alumina/aluminum prices, 2026 valuation is fair and the risk-reward is balanced.

A lift in U.S. premiums or reduction in import tariffs would reduce the cash drag for Alcoa (NYSE:AA), but de-escalation of reciprocal tariffs may not mean 25% section 232 tariffs on U.S. aluminum imports will be modified, UBS said.

data.title: Charter Communications most shorted S&P 500 communications stock in April; Alphabet least shorted
data.description:
Bets against communication services stocks rose in April compared to the end of March, with Charter Communications (CHTR) being the most shorted stock in the sector and Google-parent Alphabet (GOOGL) taking the spot for the least shorted.

Average short interest across S&P 500 communications services stocks was 1.87% of shares float in end-April, up from 1.83% at the end of March.

The S&P 500’s communications services sector (NYSEARCA:XLC) is up over 4% so far this year, compared to the 0.2% rise in the broader market.

Stocks with the largest and least short positions

Ranked by short interest as a percentage of shares float

 

Meta Platforms Class A (META), Alphabet Class A (GOOGL) and Netflix (NFLX), the three largest contributors to the index, had short interest of 1.43%, 1.19% and 1.93%, respectively.

Charter Communications had 11.69 million shares sold short as of April 30, or 12.17% of shares float.

Omnicom Group (OMC) was the second most shorted stock at 11.90% of shares float, followed by Paramount Global (PARA) and TKO Group Holdings (TKO), with short interest of 11.22% and 9.92%, respectively.

Alphabet and Walt Disney (DIS), were the least shorted stocks, which had 68.99 million and 21.44 million shares sold.

Industry Analysis

Average short interest as a percentage of floating shares

 

Media was the most shorted industry within the sector, with 3.26% short interest as of end-April, up from 3.29% during end-March.

Entertainment stocks took the second spot with 2.88% short interest as of April 30, compared to 2.67% at March-end.

Interactive Media and Services, which has stocks like Alphabet and Meta Platforms, was the least shorted industry in the sector with 1.10% short interest as of April.

data.title: Thoma Bravo sells rest of its stake in Nasdaq - report
data.description:
Private equity firm Thoma Bravo sold its remaining shares in Nasdaq (NASDAQ:NDAQ) for ~$3.4B on Tuesday, according to a media report published on Thursday.

The firm sold its 25.5M shares, a 4.4% stake in Nasdaq, at about $80.68 apiece, Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

That sale of stock followed Thoma Bravo's sale of 17.3M shares of Nasdaq (NASDAQ:NDAQ) stock at $77.90 per share on May 7.

The private equity firm had acquired a 14.9% stake in Nasdaq in 2023 when the exchange operator bought Adenza in 2023 for $10.5B in cash and stock.

The May 7 block of shares that was sold was re-offered at a range of $78.20-$78.57 per share, one person told  Bloomberg. Another person said the May 13 block was offered at ~$81 per share.

Nasdaq (NDAQ) stock edged up 0.1% in Thursday midday trading.

data.title: Epsilon Energy outlines $9M-$12M capital plan for 2025 amid 200% sequential Marcellus cash flow growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Epsilon Energy Ltd. (EPSN) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Jason Stabell, Chief Executive Officer, highlighted strong results for the quarter, primarily attributed to the Pennsylvania business, stating Marcellus upstream cash flows were up sequentially over 200% with a 58% increase in production and a 70% increase in realized pricing. He added, "midstream cash flows increased 140% sequentially on higher throughput volumes." Stabell also emphasized a cautious near-term activity plan due to oil price volatility, with intentions to invest in 0.5 net wells each in Texas and Alberta, totaling $9 million to $12 million in capital expenditures for the remainder of the year, including a $1.5 million drilling carry in Alberta, and confirmed, "We don't expect additional investments this year in Pennsylvania." He stressed, "Our strong balance sheet and projected cash flows leave us well positioned to capitalize on attractive opportunities while maintaining our dividend."

  • Andrew Williamson, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "The performance in the Marcellus this quarter demonstrates our assets leverage to incremental development there and a strong pricing environment, both on the upstream and midstream side of the business." Williamson also noted, "We don't expect incremental development there this year, but we have substantial remaining undeveloped inventory, roughly 500,000 completed lateral length feed gross, which we expect to be developed starting late next year or early in 2027."

  • Henry Clanton, Chief Operating Officer, updated on drilling activity: "In Texas, the current plan calls for two wells to be drilled to satisfy the lose development obligations. Both of these wells are expected to be 2-mile laterals or longer. The first well is planned to spud in late May with the completion planned for the third quarter. In Canada, our first two horizontal Mannville wells in the Garrington area were completed in the first quarter. Both wells are currently on production with sales commencing in April."

Outlook

  • Epsilon plans $9 million to $12 million in capital expenditures through the rest of 2025, with no additional investments anticipated in Pennsylvania and 0.5 net wells each planned in Texas and Alberta.

  • Williamson indicated that the remaining Marcellus undeveloped inventory is expected to begin development "starting late next year or early in 2027," dependent on market conditions and operator plans.

  • The company is maintaining a hedge strategy, with "approximately 45% hedged on forecasted PDP oil production for the remainder of the year from May at just over $71 WTI" and "approximately 30% hedged on forecasted PDP production for the same period at $3.33 in NYMEX."

Financial Results

  • Marcellus upstream cash flows rose over 200% sequentially, attributed to both a 58% increase in production and a 70% increase in realized pricing.

  • Midstream cash flows increased 140% sequentially, benefiting from higher throughput volumes.

  • No explicit quarterly EPS or revenue figures were stated in the transcript.

Q&A

  • John White, Roth Capital: "You said there's not going to be any more development drilling in Marcellus this year. Do you want to offer any additional details on the first two wells in Alberta and how those are doing?" Jason Stabell replied, "A little early to offer too much there. Other than to say we've got oil and gas started flowing that to sales in early April...we're producing cash flow up there right now, but we're really going to have more definitive comments as we march through the second quarter into the third quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on production and development timelines, particularly in Alberta and Marcellus, with a neutral tone, as questions sought clarification rather than expressing doubt or concern.

  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing strong operational results and financial flexibility. In response to analyst queries, the tone became more cautious regarding the Alberta wells, as Stabell said, "A little early to offer too much there."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from discussing strategic expansion to highlighting operational outperformance and a more conservative approach to capital allocation. Analyst sentiment remained neutral in both quarters.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In the previous quarter, management emphasized strategic objectives, new project areas, and a resilient approach to market headwinds, while this quarter highlighted operational outperformance, especially in Marcellus, and a more disciplined capital plan.

  • Guidance language shifted from expectations of increased development in Alberta and the Permian to a focus on limiting capital spending and cautious near-term activity.

  • Analysts in both quarters concentrated on production results, development plans, and operational updates, though this quarter saw less emphasis on hedging and share repurchase activity.

  • Key metric changes included a significant sequential rise in Marcellus cash flows and production, and a pause on further Pennsylvania investment for the year.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted "current oil price volatility" as a factor in limiting near-term activity and capital expenditures.

  • Williamson caveated development projections by stating, "the operator stated plans are, of course, subject to change based on gas market conditions and other factors."

  • Ongoing efforts to optimize production in Alberta reflect operational risks associated with early-stage well performance.

Final Takeaway

Epsilon Energy delivered robust sequential growth in Marcellus production and cash flows, driven by favorable pricing and higher volumes, while signaling a cautious approach to capital allocation for the remainder of 2025 in light of commodity market volatility. The company intends to invest $9 million to $12 million in Texas and Alberta, with no additional Pennsylvania activity planned this year, and maintains a disciplined hedge strategy and a strong balance sheet to support future opportunities and ongoing dividend commitments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Long-term mortgage rates stay below 7% for 17th straight week
data.description:
Long-term mortgage rates remained below the 7% threshold for the 17th consecutive week, sending purchase application activity higher, according to the Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.81% as of May 15, up from 6.76% last week but below 7.02% in the year-ago week.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.92%, up from 5.89% last week and down from 6.28% a year ago.

"Stable mortgage rates coupled with moderately rising inventory are attracting homebuyers into the market, with purchase application activity up 18% from last year," said Freddie Mac's chief economist, Sam Khater.

data.title: ‘Volatility is the name of the game’ from now on – MetLife’s chief market strategist
data.description:
The U.S. is still getting a recession, according to Drew Matus, MetLife Investment Management chief market strategist.

During a CNBC interview, Matus said he continues to anticipate a recession and stated that volatility is “the name of the game going forward.”

He pointed to the impact of equity market volatility (VIX) on upper-income consumers, who have been a key driver of spending. This volatility can create a “hot stove mentality” or wealth effect, leading these consumers to potentially pull back on consumption.

Companies also face significant uncertainty due to tariff rates that could change depending on manufacturing location. “This makes it difficult for firms to plan investments and manage costs,” potentially leading them to consider how to save money, which can translate to staffing decisions, he said.

Survey data suggests upper-income consumers are increasingly concerned about job security and expect lower real wage gains. This prompts them to “start saving for a rainy day,” leading to reduced spending on non-essentials like dining out or vacations, Matus added.

He believes these behavioral shifts, if widespread enough, can become “a self-fulfilling prophecy toward slower economic activity.”

He noted that survey data indicates consumers are becoming increasingly fearful for the outlook at an unusual speed.

While a formal recession may or may not occur, Matus concluded that whatever economic conditions the market is heading into are unlikely to “feel all that great.” Consumer fear is a key factor, setting the stage for a significant slowing in growth.
